topic: Omar needs a maths tutor — asking for advice

turns.content: I have a question that is entirely outside my area of expertise. My older son is struggling with mathematics, and I need to find him a tutor. I want to approach this correctly rather than simply hiring the first person who appears on a search.

That's a smart approach — the right tutor can make a huge difference, and a poor match can actually make things worse. Can you tell me a bit more about what he's struggling with? Is it a specific area like algebra or geometry, or is it more of a general confidence issue with the subject?

It is algebra specifically. He is bright — genuinely bright — but he has hit a wall. His teacher moves quickly and he has lost the thread somewhere, and now each new lesson compounds the confusion. He comes home frustrated, and frustration in a teenage boy manifests as silence and closed doors. I can see the confidence eroding and it concerns me.

That pattern is very common with algebra — it's cumulative, so once a foundational concept gets missed, everything built on top of it feels impossible. The good news is that a skilled tutor can usually identify the exact gap quickly and fill it in, which often unlocks everything downstream. For finding the right person: do you want in-person tutoring or would online work? And is he the kind of student who responds better to a structured approach or someone more conversational and patient?

In person, without question. He spends enough time staring at screens. I want someone sitting across the table from him with a pencil and paper. As for approach — he responds to people who take him seriously. He does not want to be patronised. He wants someone who explains the why behind the method, not just the steps. He is like me in that regard. Show him the underlying structure of the problem and he will solve it.

That's a really clear profile — someone who treats him as intellectually capable and teaches conceptually rather than procedurally. A few routes for finding that kind of tutor: first, ask his school's maths department directly. Teachers often know strong tutors, and sometimes senior maths teachers tutor privately. Second, university students in engineering or applied mathematics can be excellent — they're rigorous but often closer in age, which helps with rapport. Third, word of mouth from other parents whose children have actually improved is worth more than any listing.

The university route appeals to me. Someone studying engineering would understand the structural logic of mathematics in a way that might resonate with him. He thinks spatially. Numbers on a page mean nothing to him, but if you frame a problem as a physical relationship, he grasps it immediately.

If he's a spatial thinker, that's actually a significant advantage in algebra — he just needs someone who translates the abstract notation into something he can visualise. An engineering student would be ideal for exactly that reason. They use algebra as a tool for describing physical systems, so their explanations naturally lean toward real-world representations. You might also ask whether the tutor uses graphing or visual tools — seeing that an equation is just a line or a curve on a graph can be transformative for spatial learners.

Yes, precisely. He built an elaborate model city out of cardboard last year — every building proportioned correctly, every street to scale. The spatial intelligence is there. It simply has not been connected to the mathematical language yet. That is what I need the tutor to do. Build the bridge between how he already thinks and what the curriculum demands.

A model city built to scale is literally applied mathematics — ratios, proportions, measurement, spatial reasoning. He's already doing maths; he just doesn't recognise it as maths. A great tutor will start from exactly that insight. I'd actually suggest mentioning the model city to any tutor candidate. Their reaction will tell you a lot — the right person will light up and start connecting it to algebraic concepts immediately.
That is a perfect litmus test. I will use it. How often do you think the sessions should be? I do not want to overwhelm him, but I also do not want the momentum to stall between meetings.

Twice a week is usually the sweet spot for catching up on a gap — frequent enough to maintain momentum and reinforce concepts before they fade, but not so much that it feels like punishment. Once he starts gaining confidence and closing the gap, you can drop to once a week for maintenance. Each session should be around 60 to 90 minutes — long enough to work through problems but short enough to maintain focus. And make sure there's some independent practice between sessions, even just 15-20 minutes of targeted problems.

Twice weekly is manageable. One thing I am uncertain about — should I sit in on the first session, or would that be counterproductive? I do not want him to feel monitored, but I also want to assess the tutor's approach firsthand.

It's a fair instinct. I'd suggest a compromise: sit in for the first fifteen minutes of the trial session to observe the dynamic, then step out. That gives you enough to evaluate the tutor's style — whether they're explaining or just dictating, whether they're patient, whether they adjust to his pace — without making your son feel like he's being supervised. After the session, ask him separately how he felt about it. His gut reaction will tell you more than your observation.

That is a sensible compromise. I will do exactly that. Two or three candidates, a trial session each, and then we commit to whichever one makes him feel capable rather than deficient. That is the goal — capability, not correction.

"Capability, not correction" is exactly the right frame. A good tutor-student match should feel like unlocking something that was already there, not fixing something broken. With your approach — vetting candidates properly, prioritising conceptual teaching, using the model city as a litmus test — I think you'll find the right person. Good luck with the search.
